segments.s152-g9: همه تلخی از بهر بیشی بود = 'All the bitterness was because of excess.' همه (pre-nominal quantifier) modifies تلخی and here means 'all'/'the whole (of)'; تلخی is an abstract noun 'bitterness' (feeling). از بهر is a causal phrase ('because of' / 'on account of'), where از is a preposition and بهر (literary) means 'for/for the sake of' and combines to mark cause. بود is the simple past/copula 'was' linking the predication.
segments.s152-g10: مبادا که با آز خویشی بود = 'May it not be that kinship was motivated by greed.' مبادا is a literary optative/negative wish meaning 'may it not be' / 'lest'; که introduces the subordinate clause. با here functions as 'by/with' marking the motivating factor (with greed), آز is 'greed', خویشی is 'kinship/affinity', and بود is the past copula 'was' — the whole clause expresses a fear/denial that kinship was based on greed.
segments.s152-g11: وزان روی سهراب با انجمن = 'And from that appearance Sohrab (among) the company...' وزان is a literary connective meaning 'and from that / because of that'; روی here means 'appearance/face' (the sight or aspect); سهراب is the proper name, and با انجمن places him 'with the assembly/company' — word order follows classical Persian (connective + noun phrase + adjunct).
segments.s152-g12: همی می‌گسارید با رودزن = 'he/they kept dispersing (or driving off) with the river‑robber.' همی is a literary continuous/iterative prefix meaning 'kept / would'; می‌گسارید is a present/imperfective-form verb (mi‑ + stem) with an older plural/epic ending -ید used in narrative to indicate repeated or ongoing action. با marks accompaniment/means, and رودزن (here) refers to a river‑robber; the clause says he repeatedly scattered or routed that robber among the company.
segments.s152-g13: به هومان is a prepositional phrase marking the indirect object ('to Hōmān'). چنین گفت is a fixed narrative phrase 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past 'said'); کاین is the archaic fused form of که + این ('that/that this') introducing the following clause and linking back to شیرمرد ('this brave man'). Word order follows Classical Persian narration where the reporting verb appears before the relative clause.
segments.s152-g14: که is the relative pronoun ('who/that') introducing the clause modifying شیرمرد. با من is the accompaniment phrase 'with me.' همی‌گردد is a Classical-Persian present/habitual form with the old prefix همی- plus گردیدن; here it functions as 'accompanies/goes with (me)' in the sense of 'takes part alongside me.' اندر نبرد is the locative phrase 'in battle' placed after the verb, a typical ordering in poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s152-g15: ز here functions as the comparative 'than/from' (classical usage) and بالای من literally 'my height' or 'above me' (comparison basis). نیست is the negative copula 'is not.' بالاش uses the possessive suffix -ش 'his' on بالا (here 'his (height)'), and کم is the predicate adjective 'less' — together 'بالاش کم نیست' = 'his is not less (than mine)'. The clause therefore says 'he is not less (in height) than I.'
segments.s152-g16: برزم is a poetic/archaic form (effectively 'به رزم' = 'in battle') introducing the setting. اندرون دل is the noun phrase 'inside (his) heart' and ندارد is the negative auxiliary 'has not' (third person singular). دژم is an adjective 'sullen/gloomy' serving as the predicate: 'within his heart there is no gloom.' Note: برزم is an idiomatic, contracted poetic form rather than one of the lemma senses listed, so it is here analyzed as 'in battle.'
segments.s152-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate time clause. خورشید تابان is the noun phrase 'the shining sun' (noun + adjective). برآورد is a past/formal poetic verb here meaning 'lifted/raised' (an older literary perfective), and پر is the direct object 'feather/wing' — together giving 'when the shining sun raised its wing.' Word order is Subject (خورشید تابان) + Verb (برآورد) + Object (پر), which is typical in older Persian poetry.
segments.s152-g2: This clause describes the reaction: سیه زاغ پران is a nominal phrase 'the black, winged rook/crow' (سیه = black; زاغ = rook/crow; پران = winged/feathered). فرو برد is a compound verbal idea 'to bring down/lower' (فرو = downwards, برد here functions as the verb 'to take/bring' in poetic usage), and سر is the object 'head.' The literal order is Agent (سیه زاغ پران) + Verb (فرو برد) + Object (سر).
segments.s152-g3: تهمتن is a proper name/epithet (Tahmātan/Rostam). بپوشید is a past/formal verb 'put on' or 'assumed' (here 'donned'), used transitively. ببربیان is a noun meaning 'silence' or 'keeping silence' and functions as the object/complement 'assumed silence' (literally 'put on silence').
segments.s152-g4: نشست is the simple past 'he sat down.' از بر is an older locative/partitive expression ('on/upon' or 'from upon') marking position; together with the following nouns it gives the place where he sat. ژنده modifies پیل as 'ragged' (ژنده پیل = the ragged elephant [hide/back]), and ژیان here functions as a noun in apposition/qualification (literally 'of life' but here part of the compound phrase describing the beast). The sequence is Verb (نشست) + locative phrase (از بر ژنده پیل ژیان).
segments.s152-g5: کمندی is the object 'a noose/lasso.' به فتراک is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto the saddle-cloth' (به = to, فتراک = saddle-cloth). بر بست is a compound verbal idea 'tied/fastened upon' (بر = on/upon used with بست 'bound/fastened'); شست follows as an instrumental noun 'with the thumb' (shifts the action's manner: he fastened it using his thumb). The verb is past (بست), and the instrument appears after it.
segments.s152-g6: یکی is an indefinite pronoun 'someone' or 'one.' تیغ هندی is a noun phrase 'an Indian sword' (تیغ = sword, هندی adjectival 'Indian' or 'Indian-style'). گرفته is a past participle/verb 'seized/taken,' and بدست is an adverbial phrase 'in hand' (i.e., 'grasped in hand'). The order is Subject/indefinite (یکی) + Object (تیغ هندی) + Verb/participle (گرفته) + manner/adverbial (بدست).
segments.s152-g7: بیامد is the past tense 'he came.' بران is an adverbial/formal demonstrative prepositional element 'to/onto that' (thereupon/onto that spot). دشت is 'plain' and آوردگاه is an appositive noun 'place of arraying/field of battle' (battlefield). The phrase reads Verb (بیامد) + place specification (بران دشت آوردگاه).
segments.s152-g8: نهاده is a past participle/verb 'placed' or 'having put' (here simple past). به سر marks the location 'on/onto the head.' بر ز آهن کلاه is the noun phrase 'a helmet of iron' where ز is the poetic genitive/partitive 'of/from' and بر effectively places the helmet 'upon' — together giving 'placed upon his head an iron helmet.' Word order is verb (نهاده) + locative (به سر) + object (بر ز آهن کلاه).
segments.s152-g81: نشست is a past‑tense 3rd‑singular verb meaning 'sat' or 'settled' (archaic/poetic simple past). The phrase از بر + noun is a poetic locative (here 'upon/on') so از بر سینه پیلتن = 'upon the chest of Piltan'; Persian allows verb before its locative complement in this elevated style.
segments.s152-g82: پر از is a fixed adjectival phrase meaning 'full of'. What follows (خاک چنگال و روی و دهن) is a coordinated noun phrase specifying what it is full of; خاک here means 'dust' and modifies the list of body parts (its claws, face, and mouth). This is a verbless descriptive continuation tied to the previous clause.
segments.s152-g83: یکی here is an indefinite 'one / someone' introducing an actor. خنجری آبگون is a noun (خنجر 'dagger') with a postpositive adjective آبگون 'bluish/pale'; the verb برکشید (past, 3rd sg.) means 'lifted' or in context 'drew (a weapon)'. Word order is typical Persian noun + adjective before the final verb.
segments.s152-g84: همی‌خواست is an imperfective/past‑progressive form meaning 'was intending / was about to'. The rest is a direct object construction: از تن 'from the body' + سرش را 'his head (definite object marked by را)' + برید (cut) — note برید should be read as past of بریدن 'cut off'; the object precedes the verb as usual.
segments.s152-g85: به + proper name is the dative/indirect object 'to Sohrab', followed by the verb گفت (past 'said'); گفت is the reported‑speech verb (lemma list here is mismatched, so sense given as unavailable). ای is the vocative particle 'O', and یل شیرگیر is a vocative noun phrase 'hero who catches lions' (شیرگیر is a compound epithet modifying یل).
segments.s152-g86: This segment is a string of epithets in apposition to the vocative: کمندافگن (one who casts a lasso), و (and) + گرد + و + شمشیرگیر (swordsman). Persian commonly lists multiple descriptive nouns joined by و; each is an epithet of the addressed hero.
segments.s152-g87: دگرگونه‌تر is the comparative form 'more/different', and باشد here is an optative/subjunctive 'may be' or a stative 'is' used stylistically; آیین ما is the subject 'our custom/way'. The order places the predicate (be) before the noun phrase for emphasis, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s152-g88: جزین ('apart from this' / 'except this') is fronted for contrast, followed by another instance of باشد ('may be/let it be') and the noun phrase آرایش دین ما 'the adornment/array of our religion' (آرایش governs دین ما as a genitive). The construction expresses a contrast: aside from the foregoing, this would be the ornament of our faith.
segments.s152-g25: بدو گفت هومان که در کارزار = word-order is somewhat inverted: 'بدو' (to him) is a prepositional/indirect-object clitic phrase placed before the verb, 'گفت' is the past-tense verb 'said', and 'هومان' is the speaker's name. 'که' introduces the content clause ('that …'), and 'در کارزار' is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the battlefield' that attaches to the following reported clause.
segments.s152-g26: رسیدست رستم به من اند بار = 'رسیدست' is an older/perfect-form verb meaning 'has arrived/has come' (the '-ست' ending is an archaic/poetic form related to 'است'). The logical subject 'رستم' follows the verb (verb–subject order). 'به من' is the beneficiary/target 'to me' or 'toward me'. 'اند بار' is a frequency/quantity phrase here (literally 'occasions/times') meaning 'several times' or 'on more than one occasion.'
segments.s152-g27: شنیدم که در جنگ مازندران = 'شنیدم' is the simple past 'I heard'; 'که' introduces the indirect question/report clause. 'در جنگ مازندران' is a locative phrase 'in the (battle of) Mazandaran' specifying where the reported actions took place.
segments.s152-g28: چه کرد آن دلاور به گرز گران = 'چه کرد' is an indirect interrogative 'what did … do' or 'what … has done' (used after 'شنیدم که'). 'آن دلاور' is 'that brave one / that hero' (demonstrative + epithet). 'به' here functions as an instrumental/prepositional marker 'with/by means of', and 'گرز گران' is the noun + adjective 'heavy mace' (noun followed by adjective).
segments.s152-g29: بدین رخش ماند همی رخش اوی = 'بدین' (به + این) means 'with/at this' or 'by this'; 'رخش' is 'rakhsh' (a war-steed); 'ماند' is the past/stative 'remained' and 'همی' is an emphatic/adverbial particle 'still/indeed'; the second 'رخش اوی' repeats/identifies 'his rakhsh' (اوی = his/its). The line therefore states that, by/with this steed, his own steed remained (i.e. stayed as well / resembled/stayed behind).
segments.s152-g30: ولیکن ندارد پی و پخش اوی = 'ولیکن' is the adversative 'but/yet'; 'ندارد' is the present negative 'does not have/possess'; 'پی و پخش' are two nouns joined by 'و' (here probably names of horse‑parts or traces—'پی' can mean 'foot/track' and 'پخش' is a nominal element whose exact anatomical/technical meaning is old/poetic), and 'اوی' is the third‑person possessive 'his/its'. Grammatically the phrase says he/it lacks those things belonging to him/it.
segments.s152-g31: به شبگیر چون بردمید آفتاب = this is a poetic clause: 'به شبگیر' characterizes the sun as 'night‑chaser' (one who dispels the night); 'چون' is a temporal conjunction 'when/as'; 'بردمید' here carries the sense 'took away / removed' (i.e. the sun that takes away the night), and 'آفتاب' is the noun 'the sun' (subject). The clause thus means 'when the sun that drives off the night rose.'
segments.s152-g32: سر جنگجویان برآمد ز خواب = 'سر' literally 'head' here used metonymically for 'attention/heads'; 'جنگجویان' is 'the warriors'; 'برآمد' is the past 'arose/rose up' (they lifted); and 'ز خواب' is a prepositional phrase 'from sleep' (ز = from). Together: 'the warriors raised their heads / awoke from sleep.'
segments.s152-g65: مرا آرزو بد is an archaic/poetic clause: مرا (mara) is the accusative clitic 'me/to me' (preposing the object), آرزو is the noun 'wish', and بد here is a compressed/poetic verbal form meaning 'gave' or 'caused' (classical Persian often uses short forms). که begins the subordinate clause. در بسترت = در + بستر + ت (in + 'bed' + 2nd-person suffix) = 'in your bed'; the possessive suffix -ت is attached to بستر.
segments.s152-g66: برآید is a jussive/optative/3rd-person verbal form meaning 'may arise/come to be' or 'might happen.' به هنگام is a fixed prepositional phrase 'at the time of' (به + هنگام). هوش here means 'awareness / coming-to-senses.' از + برت = از (from) + بر + ت (the preposition/word بر with 2nd-person suffix) – here 'from you' / 'away from you.'
segments.s152-g67: کسی 'someone/anyone' is the indefinite subject. کز is the classical fused form of 'که از' meaning 'who/from whom' (here 'who remains of'). تو is the 2nd-person pronoun. ماند is the verb 'remains / is left' (perfective/simple past or present depending on context). ستودان is the infinitive/verbal noun 'to praise' and کند is the auxiliary/main verb 'does/will do' — together ستودان کند = 'will praise' or 'will utter praise.'
segments.s152-g68: This line actually contains two coordinated actions. بپرد is a present/epic tense '(will) fly away / depart' (here used of the soul). روان is 'soul/spirit' and is the subject of بپرد; تن is 'body' and belongs to the following clause. به زندان کند is a clause where به زندان 'into prison' is the goal phrase and کند 'makes/puts' is the verb: 'and (they) put the body into prison.' In summary: 'the soul departs, and the body is put in prison.'
segments.s152-g69: اگر introduces a conditional clause. هوش تو = 'your intellect/wits' (possessive order: noun + pronoun). زیر دست is an idiom meaning 'under the hand' = 'under control / subject to the authority of.' منست is an older fused/copular form meaning 'is mine / belongs to me' (archaic poetic phrasing: من + است compressed), so the whole clause means 'if your mind is under my control.'
segments.s152-g70: به فرمان یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'by the command of God' (به = by/in accordance with, فرمان = command, یزدان = God). بساییم is a classical/poetic verb form (1st-person plural optative/intentional) meaning 'we shall / we will set ourselves to serve / undertake.' دست here is literal 'hand' used idiomatically with بساییم (i.e. 'to set hand to [the work]'). The clause expresses willingness to act under God's command.
segments.s152-g71: از + اسپان = 'from the horses' (از marks source). اسپان is the plural of 'اسب' (horses; here 'war-horses' because of the modifier). جنگی modifies اسپان as an adjective 'war-/military' (so 'war-horses'). فرود آمدند is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'they alighted / dismounted' (فرود = descent/alighting, آمدند = they came). Together: 'they dismounted from the war-horses.'
segments.s152-g72: هشیوار is an adjective 'alert / watchful' (here describing them or one of them). با + گبر is the prepositional phrase 'with the gabr' (گبر = Zoroastrian / infidel). و خود = 'and himself/itself' or 'and he himself' (خود is the emphatic/reflexive pronoun). آمدند is the plural past 'they came.' Word order lists the manner/condition (هشیوار) then companions (با گبر و خود) then the verb.
segments.s152-g33: بپوشید is a classical epic past-tense verb (verb before subject), here meaning 'put on / donned.' The verb precedes the name سهراب (subject). خفتان is a participial/poetic reference to having been asleep (lit. 'from sleep' / 'the sleeper') and modifies or gives background to the action; رزم is the direct object/scope (i.e. war/arms — 'war-gear' in context). Word order V + Subject + (participial phrase) + Object is common in Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s152-g34: This is two parallel predicative phrases joined by و. Each uses the pattern [Noun+possessive] + پر + ز + Noun to mean 'X is full of Y': سرش 'his head' + پر 'full (of)' + ز رزم 'of battle'; دلش 'his heart' + پر + ز بزم 'of revelry/feast.' ش is the enclitic third‑person possessive attached to سر and دل.
segments.s152-g35: بیامد is past tense 'he came' (verb-first poetic order). خروشان is an adverbial/participial qualifier 'roaring/tumultuous' describing the manner of his coming. بران is a demonstrative/adverbial 'onto/thereupon' (lit. 'on that'), followed by دشت جنگ 'the plain of battle' (compound noun: 'plain' modified by 'battle').
segments.s152-g36: This phrase gives the instrument/hold: به + چنگ اندرون literally 'into the inner grip/hand' — i.e. 'in his inner grasp.' چنگ here is figurative (grip/hand), اندرون 'within/inner.' گرزه گاورنگ is a noun (mace) followed by a color adjective (گاورنگ 'tawny'), the adjective following the noun as is common in Persian poetry. Overall: 'with a tawny mace in his grasp.'
segments.s152-g37: ز رستم marks the addressee/target of the question ('of/from Rostam' = 'to Rostam'). بپرسید is past 'asked' (verb) and here stands before the descriptive phrase خندان دو لب 'with lips smiling' (participial adjective + noun), a compact poetic way to indicate manner (i.e. he asked, smiling). Word order is verb + object/target + manner phrase.
segments.s152-g38: تو گفتی is second-person past 'you said' (reported speech). که introduces the reported clause 'that'; با او به هم بود is an idiomatic sequence where با او 'with him' + به هم بود 'was together' (i.e. 'were together'); شب is the time adverb 'night.' So the clause means 'you said that you were with him at night.' Note: some dictionary senses given in the word list don't include the verbal sense 'you said' for گفتی, so that specific lexical sense is not listed there.
segments.s152-g39: که introduces continuation of reported speech. شب چون بدت is a poetic way to report 'when night was for you / when you spent the night' (چون here functions as a temporal 'when'). روز چون خاستی = 'when day rose / when day came you rose' (خاستی is second‑person past 'you rose'). The clause ties night and day together as the times you spent/rose.
segments.s152-g40: ز introduces origin/reason ('from / because of') and پیگار means 'battle/struggle'; بر دل literally 'upon the heart' (i.e. in your heart/mind). چه here is an interrogative 'what/how', and آراستی (second‑person past) means 'did you adorn / did you arrange' — together forming a question: 'What (of the battle) did you set upon your heart?' — i.e. 'what did you make of the fight in your heart?'
segments.s152-g57: نه ... گر introduces a contrast/negation followed by a conditional concessive: نه من کودکم‌گر = “No — (even) if I am a child.” من is the first-person pronoun. کودکم‌گر is the fused phrase کودکم + گر (گر = ‘if’/‘even if’) giving the concessive clause. تو هستی جوان follows as a separate clause meaning “you are young”; here the verb ‘هستی’ is the copula ‘you are.’
segments.s152-g58: به کشتی کمر بسته‌ام بر میان = a single predicative clause. به + کشتی = “for/into wrestling” (کشتی here = ‘wrestling’). کمر بسته‌ام is a perfect construction with the verb بسته‌ام (I have bound/tied) meaning “I have girded my waist/loins.” بر میان = “upon / in the middle” (literally ‘on the middle’), indicating location of the wrestling.
segments.s152-g59: بکوشیم is the hortatory/subjunctive first‑person‑plural form meaning “let us strive/try.” و simply links clauses. فرجام کار آن بود is a nominative verbless clause with بود (past ‘was’) as main verb: “the outcome/end (فرجام) of the matter (کار) was that….”
segments.s152-g60: که introduces the subordinate clause ‘that …’. فرمان و رای = two coordinated nouns ‘command and counsel/decision.’ جهانبان is a compound noun ‘world‑guardian’ (epithet/title). بود is the past copula ‘was’ tying the subordinate clause to فرجام کار آن بود.
segments.s152-g61: بسی گشته‌ام در فراز و نشیب = perfect/perfective statement: گشته‌ام (I have gone/been) with بسی quantifying (‘much/often’) and در فراز و نشیب = ‘through highs and lows’ (فراز = heights/summit, نشیب = descents). The verb+locative phrase describes experience of alternating fortunes.
segments.s152-g62: نیم مرد گفتار و بند و فریب is a nominal phrase describing the speaker. نیم مرد = ‘half‑man’ (i.e. only partly a man / not fully heroic). گفتار (speech), بند (here = stanza/verse line), و فریب (and deception) are three coordinated nouns that characterize what kind of ‘half‑man’ he is — a man of words, of verse, and of trickery.
segments.s152-g63: بدو گفت سهراب = a simple reporting clause: بدو = ‘to him/unto him,’ گفت here functions as ‘said’ (past) and سهراب is the speaker’s name placed after the verb (common in Persian word order in poetry). کز مرد پیر begins the reported content with کز (= archaic contraction of که + از / ‘that/from which’ or simply ‘that’ + genitive sense) introducing ‘that an old man…’.
segments.s152-g64: نباشد سخن زین نشان دلپذیر = the reported clause. نباشد is the negative form of باشد (here asserting ‘is not’/‘is not the case’ rather than an imperative), سخن = ‘speech/words,’ زین نشان = ‘by/from this sign/token’ (زین = archaic ‘از این’), and دلپذیر = ‘pleasant/agreeable.’ Literally: ‘(it) is not the case that the speech is pleasant by this sign.’
segments.s152-g41: ز کف is a prepositional phrase 'from the palm' (ز = poetic 'from', کف = 'palm of the hand'). بفگن is an archaic/poetic imperative/optative equivalent to modern بفکن ('throw, cast away'), commanding someone to discard something. این گرز و شمشیر کین is the object: the demonstrative این 'this' followed by a coordinated noun phrase 'mace and sword' with کین postpositive and descriptive ('of vengeance/hatred') applying to the weapon(s).
segments.s152-g42: بزن is an imperative 'strike' (command). جنگ و بیداد را is the coordinated definite object marked by را (object marker) meaning 'war and oppression' as the things to be struck. بر زمین is a directional/locative phrase 'to/on the ground' (بر = on/upon; زمین = ground/earth), indicating where they should be cast down.
segments.s152-g43: نشینیم is a first-person-plural jussive/optative 'let us sit' (or a hortative 'we should sit'). هر دو پیاده combines هر دو 'both' with پیاده 'on foot/dismounted' to say that both will be on foot. به هم here means 'together' (prepositional/phraseal use combining به + هم).
segments.s152-g44: به introduces an instrumental/associative phrase 'with' or 'by means of'. می‌تازه is a noun-like/participial idea 'fresh (wine)' or 'new wine' (literally 'that which becomes fresh'); داریم is present 'we have' (or 'we will have / we shall take' in this hortatory sequence). روی دژم is a noun phrase 'a grim/sullen face' (روی = face; دژم = sullen/grim) describing the expression they will put on.
segments.s152-g45: به پیش جهاندار is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the sovereign' (به + پیش = 'to/in front of', جهاندار = 'sovereign, lord'). پیمان کنیم is a first-person-plural jussive/subjunctive 'let us make a covenant' (کنیم = 'let us do/make'). The whole segment expresses the act of swearing a pledge publicly.
segments.s152-g46: دل is the (affected) 'heart' (subject or object of the following causative action). از جنگ جستن is a verbal-nominal phrase 'from seeking war' (از = 'from', جنگ = 'war', جستن = infinitival 'to seek/pursue'). پشیمان کنیم is a causative/optative construction 'let us make (it) regret' or 'let us cause (the heart) to be regretful' — i.e. resolve to turn the heart away from seeking war. کنیم is the same jussive 'let us do'.
segments.s152-g47: همان is a discourse particle here ('for now' / 'meanwhile' / 'so then') used to introduce what follows. تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'until'. کسی دیگر آید is a jussive/subjunctive 'someone else come' (آید = 'may come' / 'comes'). به رزم is a directional phrase 'to the fight/battle' (به = to, رزم = battle).
segments.s152-g48: تو با من بساز is an imperative addressed 'you': تو 'you', با من 'with me', بساز is imperative of ساختن 'make' / idiomatically 'make peace' or 'be reconciled'. و is the coordinating 'and'. بیارای بزم is an imperative phrase: بیارای is an archaic/poetic imperative of 'arrange/adorn/prepare' (here 'adorn/prepare'), and بزم is the object 'banquet/feast' (bāzm = assembly/banquet).
segments.s152-g89: کسی is the indefinite subject 'someone/whoever'. کاو here is an archaic relative element (effectively 'که او' = 'who/that') — it is not the verb 'to search' listed in the senses. بکشتی is a fused prepositional phrase 'به کشتی' meaning 'onto/by ship' (poetic contraction), and نبرد آورد is a past-tense verbal phrase: آورد is third-person past 'brought' and نبرد is its object 'battle'.
segments.s152-g90: سر مهتری is a genitival/possessive phrase 'the head of the chief' (سر = head, مهتری = the chief/elder). زیر گرد آورد is a verbal cluster: زیر = 'under/beneath' and گرد آورد functions as a compound verbal idea 'to turn/bring (something) under'; here آورد is the lexical verb 'brought' and گرد is part of the verbal phrase. The whole clause means 'brought the chief's head under/down'.
segments.s152-g91: نخستین is an adjectival/nominal 'the first' or 'firstly'. که introduces a relative/concessive clause 'who/when'. پشتش is پشت + enclitic ش 'his back'. نهد is the present/poetic third-person singular of نهاد 'to place, to set' meaning 'lays/puts'. بر زمین is the prepositional phrase 'upon the ground'. Grammatically this gives 'when/if he first lays his back upon the ground...'.
segments.s152-g92: نبرد here is the noun 'battle/struggle' placed before سرش (the phrase order is poetic). سرش is 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش). گرچه introduces a concessive clause 'although / even if'. باشد is the subjunctive/present form of 'to be' used in the concessive sense 'be/are' (poetic usage rather than the imperative sense listed). به کین is a prepositional phrase 'with/filled by vengeance'. The clause reads 'although his head be with vengeance' (i.e. though he nurse a grudge).
segments.s152-g93: گرش here functions idiomatically with بار دیگر: بار دیگر = 'once more / again' (بار = occasion/time, دیگر = again/other). The sequence به زیر آورد is the verbal phrase 'brought (him) under/down' (به = to/toward, زیر = under, آورد = brought). The clause means 'again he brought (him) beneath/down'.
segments.s152-g94: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. افگندنش is افگندن + enclitic ش: the verb 'to throw/cast' with a pronominal/clitic object — 'throwing him/down'. نام شیر آورد is 'brought/earned the name “Shir” (Lion)' — نام = 'name', شیر = 'lion', آورد = 'brought/earned'. The whole clause says that by casting him down he won the epithet 'Lion'.
segments.s152-g95: بدان here is a fused/demonstrative prepositional idea 'to that / by that' (written here as prefix ب + دان). چاره = 'remedy/means' — 'by that means'. از چنگ آن اژدها is a prepositional phrase 'from the claw of that dragon' (از = from, چنگ = claw/talon, آن = that, اژدها = dragon). The segment sets up 'by that device/from that means (to free himself) from the dragon's claw'.
segments.s152-g96: همی‌خواست is a habitual/past-intent form meaning 'he was wishing/intended' or 'he sought'. کاید is a noun/verb meaning 'a stratagem/plot' (here 'a device/plan'). ز کشتن = 'from being killed' (ز = from, کشتن = killing). رها = 'free/relieved' (object complement). Together the clause means 'he sought by that artifice to be freed from being killed.'
segments.s152-g129: همی is a frequentative/adverb meaning 'continually' or 'kept on'. خواست is a past-form verb 'he wanted/wished' (here describing an ongoing desire). پیروزی و دستگاه are coordinated objects: 'victory and دستگاه' (here 'دستگاه' = rule/establishment/power), placed after the verb as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s152-g130: نبود is the negative past copula 'was not'. آگه is a predicate adjective meaning 'aware' ('not aware'). از introduces the object of awareness ('aware of …'), and بخشش here means 'a grant' or 'bestowal' rather than 'forgiveness'. هور و ماه refer to heavenly luminaries (literally 'splendor/sun and moon').
segments.s152-g131: که چون رفت is a temporal clause 'for/that when he went/departed'. خواهد is the modal/future 'will/would'. سپهر is the subject 'the sky/heavens'. از + برش here is a compact phrase meaning 'from/over him' (برش reflects 'بر + ش' = 'upon/his'); the clause sets up that the heavens will act toward him.
segments.s152-g132: بخواهد is a reiteration of the modal 'will/would wish/attempt' and here introduces the infinitive object ربودن 'to snatch away'. کلاه 'hat' is the direct object of ربودن, and از سرش 'from his head' uses از = 'from' + سر 'head' + ش enclitic = 'his'. The structure is '[heaven] will seek to snatch the hat from his head.'
segments.s152-g133: وزان is a fused connective 'and/from that (thereafter)'. آبخور is 'watering-place' (a place to drink). شد is the copula 'became', and به جای نبرد means 'in place of battle' or 'taking the place of fighting' — the phrase says the watering-place assumed the role/location of the battlefield (poetic compression of circumstance).
segments.s152-g134: پراندیشه is an adjective 'full of thought / careworn', modifying the following nouns. بودش is the past copula with the enclitic 'ش' ('it was to him' → 'he was/it was for him'), linking to دل و روی زرد: 'his heart and face [were] anxious and pale.' دل و روی are coordinated parts ('heart and face'); زرد here means 'pale/wan'.
segments.s152-g135: همی‌تاخت is a continuous past 'kept charging/rushing' (همی- marks ongoing/action in progress). سهراب is the subject. چون پیل مست is a simile 'like an elephant, drunk/intoxicated' — a vivid epic comparison describing his ferocious, heedless charge.
segments.s152-g136: This is a terse instrument/location phrase: کمندی 'a lasso' + به بازو 'on/around the arm' and کمانی 'a bow' + به دست 'in (his) hand'. The nouns are juxtaposed to list Sohrab's weapons/gear; به marks the locative relation in each pair.
segments.s152-g97: This is a nominal phrase with an epithet plus a prepositional idiom: دلیر جوان = 'the brave youth/young man' (adjective before noun). سر به گفتار پیر is literal 'head to the old man's speech' — Persian uses سر به + [noun] (here with به + گفتار + پیر) to mean 'to give one's head/ear to [someone]' i.e. to heed or submit to their words. Word order is adjective → noun → idiom.
segments.s152-g98: بداد is the narrative past verb meaning 'gave' (here: 'gave ear' with the previous idiom) though the listed sense in the metadata does not match this use (so marked -1). و links two verbal clauses. ببود is an archaic/perfective copular form (older variant of بود) meaning 'was' (narrative past), and governs the following noun phrase این سخن دلپذیر as its predicate ('this pleasant speech was/there was this pleasant speech').
segments.s152-g99: This is an enumeration: یکی 'one/first' introduces the first item; از here expresses source or cause ('from' / 'because of'), so از دلی means 'from the heart' or 'from heartfelt feeling'. و links to the next enumerated item; دوم 'the second' is followed by از زمان 'from the time/occasion' — از + noun marks the source/reason.
segments.s152-g100: سوم 'the third' (implicit in the segment) is followed by از جوانمردیش where از again marks the source ('from his chivalry'); جوانمردیش shows the possessive suffix -ش ('his') on جوانمردی (chivalry / generous, honourable conduct). بی‌گمان is a sentence-final adverb meaning 'undoubtedly'.
segments.s152-g101: رها کرد is a compound verbal phrase: رها (root) + کرد (past of کردن) = 'released/let go'. زو is a contracted poetic form of از + او = 'from him' (i.e. 'released his hand from him' / 'let him go'). و آمد به دشت is a second verb phrase 'and came/entered into the plain' (آمد past tense + به + دشت as goal). Word order follows classical Persian narrative sequence: verb ± object then conjunction then next verb phrase.
segments.s152-g102: چو introduces a simile ('as/like'). شیری is used adjectivally/nominatively as 'a lion' (leonine). The relative clause introduced by که ('which/who') modifies شیری: بر پیش آهو گذشت = 'passed before/in front of the gazelle' where بر + پیش is a poetic locative 'in front of' and گذشت is the past 'passed'.
segments.s152-g103: همی‌کرد is a progressive/narrative imperfective 'was doing' (common in epic Persian). نخچیر (an older form of نخجیر) = 'game/prey' or 'the act of hunting' and functions as the object of the verbal phrase; و links the clauses. یادش نبود is an idiomatic predication: یادش = 'his memory' (with the clitic -ش) and نبود = 'was not' → 'he did not remember.'
segments.s152-g104: ازان is the fused archaic form of از آن ('of that' / 'from that'). کس که introduces a relative clause 'the person who'; با او نبرد is 'fought/contended with him' (با = with, نبرد = battle/struggle as noun used in the clause), and آزمود is the perfect/past participial sense 'tested/tried' (here 'had tried him in battle' or 'had engaged him in combat'). The whole phrase modifies کس ('the one who had encountered him in battle').
segments.s152-g137: گرازان is a plural noun ('boars') placed first for vividness. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. بر is the preposition 'upon/on' governing the following noun. گور here is the wild ass/onager (an archaic poetic term), and نعره‌زنان is a present-participial adjective ('roaring/neighing') modifying that noun rather than a finite verb.
segments.s152-g138: سمندش is سمند + possessive suffix -ش 'his steed/warhorse'. The doubled جهان (world) is a rhetorical repetition for emphasis; راکنان is an archaic present-participle-like formation (literary) indicating causing/making (here: causing the world to move/tremble). The whole phrase attributes a tremendous, world-shaking motion to his horse.
segments.s152-g139: همی‌ماند is the older/poetic imperfective/past-continuous sense formed with the prefix همی- + ماند (here translated 'stood/was remaining'), marking a sustained state. رستم is the subject. ازو is the fused form of از + او ('from/of him'). در شگفت is a prepositional phrase 'in wonder' describing Rostam's mental state.
segments.s152-g140: ز is the poetic variant of از 'from'. پیگارش is پیگار + -ش 'his fighting/contest' (object/source). اندازه‌ها is the plural 'measures/extent(s)' (here 'the measures/scale'), and برگرفت is a past tense verb 'took up / raised / adopted' used idiomatically as 'he took/assessed' — i.e., he measured or gauged the situation.
segments.s152-g141: چو here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. سهراب شیراوژن is a compound proper name/epithet ('Sohrab, the Shiravij/Shirwanite'). او را is the object pronoun 'him' with the definite object marker را. بدید is the older past form of 'saw'.
segments.s152-g142: ز is poetic 'by/from'. باد جوانی is a metaphorical phrase 'the wind/breeze of youth' meaning youthful impulse. دلش is دل + possessive -ش 'his heart'. بردمید is a strong past verb 'carried off / snatched away' used idiomatically: the youth's impulse seized his heart.
segments.s152-g143: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus he said'. کای is the vocative particle 'O' used in direct address. رسته is the perfect/participle 'escaped/freed' (here 'you who have escaped'), از + چنگ شیر is the genitival phrase 'from the lion's claw' (چنگ 'claw' of شیر 'lion'), so the whole segment is a vocative describing the addressee as one who has escaped the lion's grasp.
segments.s152-g144: جدا مانده is a compound participial idea 'left apart / remained apart' meaning 'spared' or 'set apart'; از زخم شیر repeats the lion image: 'from the lion's wound'. دلیر is an epithet 'brave' addressing the person. Grammatically the sequence is a string of participial/attributive phrases modifying the addressee, ending in the direct address 'brave one.'
segments.s152-g73: ببستند is a past-tense, 3rd-person-plural verb ('they bound/tied'); classical Persian commonly places the verb early in the clause. بر + noun gives location/attachment ('on/upon'); سنگ اسپ here is a compound 'stone of the horse' (in epic contexts this refers to the horse-saddle/tackle), and نبرد qualifies that saddle as the 'war-' one. The whole phrase therefore means 'they fastened (it) upon the war-horse's saddle.'
segments.s152-g74: برفتند is a past 3rd-plural verb 'they departed/rode off.' هر دو ('both of them') is the subject phrase (هر = 'each/every', دو = 'two' understood from context). روان here is a noun referring to the persons/spirits (classic poetic usage), and پر ز = 'full of' (پر followed by the genitive preposition ز). گرد is the thing they are full of (dust). The clause order places the verb before the subject phrase, as is common in older Persian narrative.
segments.s152-g75: برآویختند is a past 3rd-plural verb meaning 'they sprang upon' or 'they leaped at' (bar‑āvikhtan). گرفتن is an infinitival/masdar-like complement 'to take/seize'. The initial form بکشتی is unconventional here and does not match the usual senses given; it appears as a prefixed/compound element modifying گرفتن (a stylistic/poetic formation meaning something like 'in taking/for seizing'), so its exact gloss is uncertain in the provided sense list.
segments.s152-g76: The preposition ز ('from') introduces the source (ز تن = 'from the body'). خون و خوی را = 'blood and khwī (here: kinship/relations or native disposition)' is the definite object, marked by the object marker را. فرو ریختند is the compound verb 'they poured down/let fall' (فرو + ریختند), a past 3rd-plural verb meaning they caused these things to spill out from the body.
segments.s152-g77: بزد is a past 3rd-person singular verb 'he struck/smote.' دست سهراب is the direct object in the genitive construction 'the hand of Sohrab' (دست + proper name). چون پیل مست is a simile 'like an elephant drunk/intoxicated' (چون = 'like', پیل = 'elephant', مست = 'intoxicated' used figuratively). The order verb → object → simile is idiomatic in epic narration.
segments.s152-g78: برآوردش contains the verb برآورد (here used in a physical sense 'he lifted/dragged him up') with the object suffix ـش 'him' attached. از جای = 'from the place' (از = 'from', جای = 'place'). و = 'and'. بنهاد پست = بنهاد (past of نهادن, 'he set/placed') + پست ('low' or 'beneath') meaning 'he set (him) down low'. The clause shows two successive actions: he lifted him from his place and put him down low.
segments.s152-g79: به کردار شیری = 'in the manner/behaviour of a lion' (به = 'in/by', کردار = 'deed/behaviour', شیری = 'lion-like'). که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. بر گور نر = 'upon the male gūr' where گور is the wild ass/onager (an animal often opposed to the lion) and نر marks it as male. The whole segment sets up a simile: 'as a lion that [does X] to a male onager.'
segments.s152-g80: زند is a classical present/habitual 3rd-person form here meaning 'strikes' (زند چنگ = 'strikes with its claw'). چنگ = 'claw' is the instrument. و = 'and'. گور اندر آید به سر = 'the gūr goes/enters inward/headfirst' (اندر آید = 'enters/falls into', به سر = 'by the head' — i.e. headlong). The verbs and particles follow older epic usage where vivid, pictorial verbs appear in short clauses linked by و.
segments.s152-g49: دل من (lit. “the heart of me”) is the subject; من is a possessive/agentive clitic meaning “my.” همی is a poetic/progressive particle (here: continuing / habitual sense) placed before the verb. با تو marks the person toward whom the affection is directed, مهر is the object ("love"), and آورد is the verb (past/poetic perfect "brought/has brought" used here to mean "has come to have"). Word order is subject–(adverb)–prepositional phrase–object–verb, typical of classical Persian style.
segments.s152-g50: همی again gives a continuous/ever sense. آب شرمم is a genitive phrase “the water of my shame/modesty” (شرمم = شرم + ـم, the possessive suffix “my”); به چهر is a directional phrase “to/onto the face,” and آورد is the verb "brings/comes" placed at the end. The whole clause describes a bodily/figurative effect (blushing/tears) with the verb final as in classic Persian.
segments.s152-g51: همانا is an emphatic opener "indeed/ verily." که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause. داری is 2nd-person present "you have/possess." زِ + گردان نژاد is a prepositional phrase “from/of the troop(line)/race” (ز = "from/of", گردان = "troop/host", نژاد = "lineage/race"). Word order places the verb before the prepositional origin phrase in this poetic line.
segments.s152-g52: کنی is the 2nd-person singular subjunctive/imperative of کردن/کن ("do"/"make") used here to mean "do/make/bring (to mind)." پیش من is a prepositional phrase "before me / in my presence." گوهر خویش is "your own jewel/essence": گوهر = "jewel/essence," خوی + ش is the classical combination where ش is the possessive clitic ("-self/own") referring back to the subject, so together "your own." یاد is a noun meaning "remembrance/mention" functioning as the thing to be brought by کنی ("bring/mention your jewel").
segments.s152-g53: بدو گفت رستم is the dative + verb + subject order common in older Persian: بدو = "to him," گفت (here the past tense verb "said") and رستم is the speaker. که‌ای is an archaic interrogative/copular cluster meaning "who are you?" (که = "who/that", ای = archaic copula/particle). نامجوی is a vocative/epithet "name-seeker" (one who seeks fame); it addresses the addressee. Note: the word گفت in the provided sense list was not given in its verbal sense; the context shows it is the past tense verb "said."
segments.s152-g54: نبودیم is past negative 1st/1st-person-plural "we were not" (was/were not). هرگز is the adverb "never" intensifying the negation. بدین گفت‌وگوی is a prepositional phrase "in/to this conversation" (بدین = classical "in/with this," formed from ب- + demonstrative; گفت‌وگوی = "conversation/dialogue"). Together the clause says "we were never (given to) such conversation."
segments.s152-g55: ز introduces the topic/source (here: "about/with regard to"). کشتی گرفتن is a fixed verbal noun + verb construction meaning "to wrestle/engage in wrestling" (کشتی = "wrestling," گرفتن = "to take/engage in"). سخن بود is the common literary phrase "there was talk/conversation" (سخن = "talk/speech," بود = past copula "was"). دوش at the end is an adverbial "last night," placed after the clause as is typical in poetry.
segments.s152-g56: نگیرم is a negative-present/intentional form literally "I do not take" (here: "I will not be taken/influenced"). فریب تو = "your deception/trick" (فریب = "deception", تو = "you/your"). زین در is a classical locative/demonstrative phrase meaning roughly "in this (matter)" (در = "in/within"; the provided senses for زین did not include the demonstrative use, so that item is mapped as unavailable). مکوش is a negative imperative "do not try/strive" (مکوش = "do not attempt"). The two halves form a refusal followed by an admonition: "I won't be deceived by you—don't try that here."
segments.s152-g121: Word order: typical classical Persian V‑O order here — 'داستان زد' (verb + object) means 'told/performed a tale' with زد used poetically for 'uttered/told'. 'یکی' before the verb is an indefinite subject 'a certain person / someone'. 'برین شهریار' is a prepositional phrase: بر (toward/to) + the archaic/orthographic ین (= این, 'that/this') + شهریار 'king' = 'to that king'.
segments.s152-g122: 'که' introduces reported speech/what was said. 'دشمن' is the noun 'enemy'. 'مدار' in this line functions as part of an imperative/restrictive clause meaning roughly 'do not (treat/hold)'; this is an archaic/poetic use not matched in the provided senses (see senses mapping). 'ارچه' = 'even if/although', 'خردست' = 'is small', and 'خوار' = predicate 'despised/contemptible'. Together the clause gives the admonition: 'do not despise an enemy, even if he is small.'
segments.s152-g123: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. 'رستم' is the subject. 'ز دست وی' literally 'from his hand' (ز = from; دست here means 'grasp/hold' = power/control). 'آزاد شد' is past 'became free / was freed' (شد as past auxiliary). The clause means 'when Rostam was freed from his grasp.'
segments.s152-g124: Simile introduced by بسان 'like/as'. 'یکی تیغ پولاد' = 'a single/one sword of steel' (یکی here functioning as 'one/a certain' modifying تیغ). 'شد' = 'became' — a poetic way to say his movement/force turned into (was like) a steel sword.
segments.s152-g125: خرامان is an adverb/adjective 'with a stately/proud gait'. بشد here is perfective/preterite 'he became / it happened' (poetic usage). سوی + noun = 'toward' (سوی آب روان = 'toward the flowing water'); آب روان is a compound 'flowing/running water' (آب = water, روان = flowing).
segments.s152-g126: چنان چون شده: 'چنان' + 'چون' intensify the comparison/sequence 'thus/as it was'. شده is the past participle/auxiliary 'having become/been thus'. باز یابد روان is a verbal phrase where باز 'again' + یابد (۳rd pers. present/subjunctive of یافتن) 'finds/recovers' + روان 'soul/spirit' = 'his spirit returns / is recovered.' The clause links as the result of the previous action.
segments.s152-g127: بخورد آب here is a poetic sequence-verb phrase: بخورد (literally 'to take/drink') + آب 'water' — followed by و + روی و سر و تن 'and face and head and body' and the verb بشست 'he washed'. The structure lists what he washed in coordination and places the verbal action بشست at the end (common in classical Persian).
segments.s152-g128: به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'into the presence of / before'. جهان‌آفرین is a compound 'world‑creator' (the Creator/God). شد is the past 'became/went' and نخست 'first' sets the sequence: 'he first went/stood before the Creator.' This is a classical phrasing for 'he first went to God/prayed' in the narrative order.
segments.s152-g17: This is a simple nominal list describing body-parts in the order 'بر و کتف و یالش' (back/upper part, shoulder, mane) followed by the simile 'همانند من' ('like me'). Word order is noun-noun-noun + comparator + pronoun; 'همانند' governs the following pronoun 'من' (= 'like me').
segments.s152-g18: 'تو گویی که ...' is an impersonal, archaic idiom meaning 'one would say / it seems as if'. Here 'تو' does not function as a normal second‑person pronoun, and 'گویی' is the verbal/idiomatic element meaning 'one would say' or 'it appears'. The clause then uses a simple past/preterital action 'زد' ('struck') with the object 'رسن' ('rein'), and the prepositional/adverbial 'بر' before 'زد' marks the strike as 'upon' or 'on'.
segments.s152-g19: 'نشان‌های مادر بیابم همی' places the noun phrase 'نشان‌های مادر' ('my mother's signs/tokens') first; 'بیابم' is a first‑person present/subjunctive form of یافتن ('find') and with the classical prefixal particle 'همی' indicates a continuing or hortative sense ('I shall/keep seeking' or 'may I find'). The sequence therefore reads as the speaker declaring intention or hope to find those tokens.
segments.s152-g20: 'بدان' here is the prepositional combination 'ب-' + demonstrative/pronoun ('to/at that' ≈ 'toward them'); 'نیز' = 'also'; 'لختی' = 'for a short while'; 'بتابم' is a first‑person verb from تابیدن with the sense 'turn/turn toward' (classical/poetic use), and again 'همی' gives the continuous/iterative flavor. Put together: 'I will also turn toward them briefly.'
segments.s152-g21: 'گمانی برم من' is an idiomatic way to say 'I suspect / I suppose'; 'گمانی' = 'a supposition' and 'برم' here is an older/clausal form completing that idea ('I hold' / 'I bear the thought') with the explicit subject 'من'. The following 'که او رستمست' is a complement clause 'that he is Rostam' (رستمست = 'is Rostam' in a fused poetic form).
segments.s152-g22: The subordinating 'که' introduces the reason: 'چون او بگیتی' = 'one such as he in the world' ('چون' = 'such as/like'), 'نبرده' = 'has not taken/overcome' (negative perfective), and 'کمست' = 'is scarce/rare'. The clause therefore states the reason: 'for it is rare in the world that someone like him has not (something) taken' — i.e. he has conquered almost all. Word order is standard subordinate: conjunction + topic + verb + predicate 'کمست'.
segments.s152-g23: 'نباید که من با پدر جنگجوی' uses the impersonal/modal 'نباید' ('one ought not / it should not be') plus the subordinate 'که' and the subject 'من'. 'با پدر جنگجوی' functions as a complement meaning 'fight with/against my father' (in older usage 'با' can mark the adversary). The overall meaning: 'I ought not to be a warrior against my father.'
segments.s152-g24: This continues the speaker's intended reaction. 'شوم' repeats the subjunctive/optative 'may I become' or simply completes the preceding clause ('that I become'); 'خیره‌روی' (a noun) denotes a fixed, astonished gaze — rendered here as 'be stunned/astonished'; 'اندر آرم' ('in/within I rest') is an archaic way to say 'I withdraw inside / take refuge within' (آرم = 'I rest/repose'); and 'بروی' is the present of رفتن ('go/leave'). Together: 'I will be stunned, withdraw inward, and go away.'
segments.s152-g105: همی دیر شد تا که is a temporal preface: همی (an emphatic/continuous particle) + دیر شد (“it became late / it was long”) + تا که (“until/that”), introducing the time clause that follows. چو + noun + verb (چو گرد بیامد) is the subordinate temporal clause “when he came around”; the sequence is typical of epic Persian where چو introduces a temporal 'when' clause.
segments.s152-g106: بیامد and بپرسیدش are two past narrative verbs in sequence (“he came and asked him”); the prefixed b‑ in classical/narrative Persian is a common story‑tense marker. The clitic ‑ش on بپرسیدش is the 3rd‑person object “him,” and از introduces the topic (asking about) followed by هم‌نبرد “comrade‑in‑arms.”
segments.s152-g107: به هومان بگفت = “he told Hōmān”; the indirect object به هومان appears before the verb. آن کجا رفته بود is a reported clause “where that one had gone,” with رفته بود a compound past (pluperfect) where بود functions as an auxiliary to mark past perfect.
segments.s152-g108: سخن هرچه = “the speech / whatever words” (هرچه is an indefinite relative modifying سخن). The clause رستم بدو گفته بود places the agent (رستم) and the dative clitic (بدو “to him”) before گفته بود; again گفته بود is the past perfect (“had said”).
segments.s152-g109: بدو گفت هومان = “to him Houmān said” (بدو is the indirect object clitic/phrase fronted). گرد‌ای جوان is a vocative: گرد + ای + جوان (“O Gard, young one”); in epic Persian the vocative marker ای attaches to the addressed name/epithet. Word order places the vocative after the reporting clause.
segments.s152-g110: به سیری رسیدی = “have you come to satiety / have you attained satisfaction” (رسیدی 2nd‑person past/perfect meaning 'you reached, you attained'). همانا is an emphatic particle (“indeed/verily”), and ز جان (“from life / of the soul”) gives the domain — i.e. 'sated of life' or 'tired of living' in context.
segments.s152-g111: دریغ here is an interjection/intensifier meaning “pity/alas.” The following list این بر و بازو و یال تو enumerates body parts (“this shoulder/upper arm and arm and your mane”); in poetic language بر can function as a noun (shoulder/upper part) even though the lemma list gives prepositional senses. تو follows the nouns to mark possession (‘your’).
segments.s152-g112: میان یلی = “among the valiant/warriors” (میان + noun). چنگ و گوپال تو are the two items being ascribed to him; چنگ here is the concrete sense 'claw/talon' (a metaphor of strength) and گوپال is treated as a name or epithet (Gopāl). Note that the possessive تو is postposed in this poetic line, attaching to both nouns.
segments.s152-g113: هژبری (a proper name) is the direct object/head of the clause. که introduces a relative clause ('whom/that') modifying the name. آورده بودی is the past perfect/simple past construction ('you had brought' / 'you brought'), with بودی marking past. بدام here is a fused prepositional phrase (بِ + دام = 'into the trap'); it functions as the directional/comitative complement to آورده بودی but is lexically irregular in the provided sense list.
segments.s152-g114: رها کردی is a two-word predicate: رها (release) + کردی (you did) — together 'you released/let go'. از دام is a normal prepositional phrase ('from the trap'). و links the clauses. شد here means 'became' and introduces the result clause کار خام ('the matter/affair became raw/ill-considered/ruined'), with خام qualifying کار.
segments.s152-g115: نگه کن is an imperative compound: نگه (look/attention) + کن (imperative 'do'), meaning 'look/heed'. کزین is an older fused preposition + demonstrative ('from this' = از + این with the k- prefix) introducing the source. بیهده qualifies کارکرد: 'a futile/pointless result/action', where کارکرد is used as 'result/effect' of an action.
segments.s152-g116: چه is an interrogative 'what'. آرد is a third-person singular present/imperative-old-form verb 'brings' (classical/simple present), so the clause asks 'what will it bring'. به پیشت is a locative/directional phrase 'to/for you, before you'. به دیگر نبرد is elliptical/concise poetic phrasing: دیگر 'another', نبرد here functions as 'battle' (noun), understood as 'in another battle' or 'against another [enemy]'; the clause as a whole questions the usefulness of the act in other combat.
segments.s152-g117: بگفت is the simple past 'he said'. و links the two clauses. دل is the grammatical subject of برگرفت in this idiom ('his heart'), followed by از جان او 'from his life' where از marks separation and او is the possessor/experiencer. برگرفت is a past transitive verb here meaning 'took away/removed', so the phrase idiomatically means 'his heart left his life' (i.e., he was stricken/stunned).
segments.s152-g118: پرانده is used adjectivally/participially (lit. 'flung/struck' or 'as if thrown'), describing the stunned state. همی is the old continuous particle meaning 'still' or 'continually'. ماند is past 'remained/was left'. ازو ('از او') means 'from him' or 'because of it', در is the preposition 'in', and شگفت is a noun meaning 'wonder/amazement' — together: 'he remained, as if flung, from it in wonder', i.e. 'he stood there stunned with amazement.'
segments.s152-g119: به لشکرگه خویش is a directional phrase: به + لشکرگه 'to the army-camp'. خویش is the possessive 'his own', here split orthographically into خوی + ش (خوی 'kin/own' + ش possessive suffix). بنهاد روی is a classical collocation meaning 'set/placed his face' = 'turned his face toward' (بنهاد is a past verb 'put/laid' used idiomatically with روی 'face').
segments.s152-g120: به خشم introduces the manner 'in anger'. و links the next clause. دل از غم 'the heart from sorrow' uses از to indicate cause/source. پر here is the adjective 'full (of)' (not 'feather'). از کار اوی is 'with/of that affair of his' (اوی = his); together: 'his heart, from sorrow, was full of that affair' = 'his heart was filled with sorrow about that matter.'
